critic_raw:
The methodology is well-specified in many respects — label separation from transaction date, graph temporal ordering, matched preprocessing, single test-set evaluation — but there are several genuine structural issues worth naming.

**Issue 1: Stratified random split leaks graph topology into test set**

The methodology uses a stratified *transaction-level* random split rather than a temporal split. This means accounts, devices, and counterparty relationships present in the training set are almost certainly present in the test set as well, because transactions from the same account span the entire 18–24 month window and are scattered across all three partitions. When the GNN performs message-passing on a test transaction, its neighborhood includes training-partition transactions by construction (the 90-day graph window is built from all prior transactions, not just training-partition transactions). The GNN therefore gets a richer, more complete graph representation at test time than it would in production deployment, where novel accounts would have sparser histories. The GBT does not benefit from this in the same way — rolling-window features degrade gracefully with sparse history, but graph embeddings from well-connected neighborhoods would be inflated. This creates an asymmetric advantage for the GNN in evaluation that would not hold in deployment, systematically inflating GNN AUC relative to the baseline. 

Evidence one way or the other: Compare GNN performance on test-set transactions belonging to accounts with *no* training-set transactions (true cold-start accounts) versus accounts that appear in training. If cold-start performance is substantially worse than warm-start, the graph topology leakage is materially biasing the comparison. A temporal holdout (e.g., last 3–4 months as test) would remove the confound entirely.

**Issue 2: Homophily leakage via device-sharing edges between fraud cases**

AUTHORIZED push-payment fraud frequently involves fraud rings whose accounts share devices. If ring members appear in both the training and test partitions (which is highly likely under a random transaction-level split), the device-sharing edges in the GNN graph at test-scoring time directly connect test fraud cases to labeled training fraud cases. The message-passing layers propagate the training fraud signal into the test node's embedding. This is a form of target leakage: the GNN effectively has access to the known-fraud labels of ring neighbors when scoring a test transaction. The GBT has no analogous channel because it operates on per-transaction summaries without a graph. This mechanism — not the general value of graph structure — could explain most of the hypothesized AUC gap.

Evidence: Run the evaluation with device-sharing edges removed at test time (or edges to training-partition nodes masked). If AUC drops sharply, the improvement is attributable to label propagation rather than learned graph representations. Alternatively, show that the gain persists on test transactions whose device-sharing neighbors are *exclusively* other test-partition transactions (no training-partition labeled fraud in neighborhood).

**Issue 3: Asymmetric information set — GNN receives graph features that subsume GBT features, not matched inputs**

The methodology notes the GNN receives "all baseline features plus temporal embeddings" from the graph. This means the GNN strictly dominates the GBT in feature richness. A 5-percentage-point AUC gap is the success criterion, but the experiment cannot isolate whether gains come from (a) graph structure per se, (b) additional node-level temporal features only computed for the GNN, or (c) the expressive capacity of the neural architecture even on baseline features alone. The stated goal — "the comparison isolates the value of graph structure" — is not achieved by this design. A proper ablation requires a third model: the GNN architecture trained on transaction-summary features only (no graph), establishing whether the neural architecture itself, independent of graph connectivity, drives gains.

Evidence: If GNN-no-graph (same architecture, no message-passing) substantially outperforms GBT, the result demonstrates neural architecture advantage, not graph structure advantage. The hypothesis is only confirmed if GNN-with-graph > GNN-no-graph > GBT, or GNN-with-graph > GBT and GNN-no-graph ≈ GBT.

**Issue 4: 90-day graph window boundary creates implicit future leakage for early test-period transactions under random split**

Under a random transaction-level split, a test transaction occurring in month 6 will have its 90-day graph window populated from months 3–6. Some of those month 3–6 transactions will be training-partition transactions, and their fraud labels are known to the model (via the trained node embeddings or edge weights). But more subtly: if graph construction uses *confirmed* fraud labels to weight edges or initialize node features (e.g., flagging known-fraudulent sender nodes), those labels bleed into the representation of neighboring test-node embeddings. The methodology does not specify whether node features in the graph include any label-derived signals; if they do, this is direct leakage. If they do not, this issue is mitigated, but needs explicit confirmation.

Evidence: Confirm that node features used in message-passing contain zero label-derived statistics. Any feature like "sender historical fraud rate" computed over the full dataset (not strictly the training partition) would constitute leakage.

**Summary of root cause grouping:**
- Issues 1 and 2 share the same root: random transaction-level split causes graph topology and label information from training to contaminate GNN test representations asymmetrically. Both inflate GNN AUC in a way that would not replicate in deployment.
- Issue 3 is a design confound: the experiment as stated cannot answer its own stated hypothesis because it conflates graph structure value with architecture value and feature richness.
- Issue 4 is a conditional leakage risk contingent on whether label-derived node features are used; lower severity if node features are confirmed label-free.

Issues 1 and 2 are the most serious because they affect the validity of the primary comparison metric, not just interpretability. The fix for both is a temporal holdout rather than a stratified random split — a design change, not a parameter tweak.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random transaction-level split leaks graph topology into test set, giving the GNN richer neighborhood representations at test time than would exist in production deployment, asymmetrically inflating GNN AUC.

Device-sharing edges under a random split connect test fraud cases to labeled training fraud cases, allowing message-passing to propagate training fraud labels into test node embeddings — a form of target leakage with no GBT analog.

The GNN receives all baseline features plus graph embeddings, so the experiment cannot isolate graph structure value from neural architecture expressiveness; a GNN-no-graph ablation is required to support the stated hypothesis.

If any node features in the graph include label-derived statistics computed over the full dataset rather than strictly the training partition, this constitutes direct label leakage into GNN representations.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random transaction-level split leaks graph topology into test set, giving the GNN richer neighborhood representations at test time than would exist in production deployment, asymmetrically inflating GNN AUC.

Device-sharing edges under a random split connect test fraud cases to labeled training fraud cases, allowing message-passing to propagate training fraud labels into test node embeddings — a form of target leakage with no GBT analog.

The GNN receives all baseline features plus graph embeddings, so the experiment cannot isolate graph structure value from neural architecture expressiveness; a GNN-no-graph ablation is required to support the stated hypothesis.

If any node features in the graph include label-derived statistics computed over the full dataset rather than strictly the training partition, this constitutes direct label leakage into GNN representations.
